title: confusing error message for +verilator+seed
body:
## What 'verilator' command line do we use to run your example?

compiled a testbench, ran the executable like this:
```
obj_dir/b_top +verilator+seed+2549418445
```
Got this message:
```
%Error: COMMAND_LINE:0: Argument '+verilator+seed+' must be an unsigned integer, greater than 0, less than 2147483648
Aborting...
```
First of all, why is an unsigned integer's max value not 2^32?  Second, is 0 not allowed?

In my simulation script I have this note to myself that shows that commercial simulators have confusing documentation on seeds too:
```
            # <commercial-simulator> documentation on -sv_seed says the seed is an
            # integer and, "The integer may be any signed 32-bit
            # integer (-2147483648 to +2147483647)." (page 1036 of the
            # <Commercial> Sim Command Reference Manual, v10.5).
            #
            # *HOWEVER*, <commercial-simulator> complains if I give it a negative seed and
            # converts it to a positive one (larger than 2147483647).
            # So, just use positive 32-bit integers.
```
I'm fine if verilator has different constraints on the seed, it's just the wording of the verilator error message that has me questioning.

## What 'verilator --version' are you using?  Did you try it with the git master version?  Did you try it with other simulators?

Verilator 5.045 devel rev v5.044-100-g996ae35a1

## What OS and distribution are you using?

Rocky Linux 9

## May we assist you in trying to fix this in Verilator yourself?

yes
code_blocks:

%Error: COMMAND_LINE:0: Argument '+verilator+seed+' must be an unsigned integer, greater than 0, less than 2147483648
Aborting...
            # <commercial-simulator> documentation on -sv_seed says the seed is an
            # integer and, "The integer may be any signed 32-bit
            # integer (-2147483648 to +2147483647)." (page 1036 of the
            # <Commercial> Sim Command Reference Manual, v10.5).
            #
            # *HOWEVER*, <commercial-simulator> complains if I give it a negative seed and
            # converts it to a positive one (larger than 2147483647).
            # So, just use positive 32-bit integers.
